past_medical_history: Generalized tonic-clonic seizure nine months ago, treated at outside hospital with diagnosis of presumed viral encephalitis; received course of intravenous acyclovir
Started on levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily after the seizure episode nine months ago
Hypertension diagnosed 4 years ago, well controlled on medication
No history of immunodeficiency or malignancy

medications.indication: Seizure prophylaxis after generalized tonic-clonic seizure

medications.frequency: as needed up to four times daily

family_history: Father died of prostate cancer at age 74
Mother alive at 78 with type 2 diabetes mellitus
No family history of brain tumors or neurological diseases

social_history.alcohol: Occasional social drinker, 1-2 drinks per month
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife and two children in a single-story home
social_history.exercise: Used to walk regularly, reduced over the past two weeks due to weakness

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Mildly inattentive, makes one error on serial sevens. Language is fluent with occasional word-finding difficulty. Follows three-step commands. Recalls 2/3 objects at five minutes. Judgment and insight intact.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light at 3 mm bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation. Fundoscopic exam reveals mild bilateral papilledema. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact bilaterally. Mild right lower facial droop. Hearing intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline on protrusion.
neurological_exam.motor: Left upper and lower extremity: 5/5 strength throughout. Right upper extremity: 3/5 deltoid, 3/5 biceps, 3/5 triceps, 3+/5 grip, 3/5 interossei. Right lower extremity: 3/5 hip flexion, 3+/5 knee extension, 3/5 ankle dorsiflexion. Pronator drift present on the right. No fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact bilaterally in upper and lower extremities. Proprioception intact at the great toes bilaterally. Vibration sense intact at the ankles bilaterally.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Left upper extremity 2+, left lower extremity 2+. Right upper extremity 3+, right lower extremity 3+ with an equivocal plantar response on the right. Left plantar response is flexor.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose mildly dysmetric on the right, intact on the left. Rapid alternating movements slowed on the right. Heel-to-shin intact bilaterally.
neurological_exam.gait: Ambulates with assistance. Right-sided circumduction noted. Reduced right arm swing. Mildly unsteady on tandem gait.
neurological_exam.additional: Mild bilateral papilledema on fundoscopic examination suggesting elevated intracranial pressure. No meningismus. No Romberg sign.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive worsening headache, right-sided weakness, and multiple episodes of vomiting over the past two days
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Arjun Mehta is a 52-year-old right-handed man who presents with a two-day history of progressively worsening holocranial headache, multiple episodes of projectile non-bilious vomiting, and new-onset right-sided weakness. His wife reports that approximately nine months ago, he experienced a single generalized tonic-clonic seizure at home, for which he was evaluated at an outside hospital. At that time, a non-contrast head CT reportedly showed a subtle ill-defined hyperdense area in the left high parietal region, and a diagnosis of viral encephalitis was made. He was treated with a two-week course of intravenous acyclovir and started on levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily for seizure prophylaxis. He initially improved and returned to his baseline functional status. Over the past several weeks, his wife has noticed progressive cognitive slowing, with him taking longer to complete routine work tasks and occasionally struggling to find the right words during conversations. Two days prior to presentation, he developed a severe holocranial headache that was unresponsive to acetaminophen. The headache was described as constant, pressure-like, and worst in the early morning hours. The following day he had three episodes of projectile vomiting without nausea. On the morning of presentation, his wife noticed that he was having difficulty lifting his right arm and was dragging his right leg while walking. She also observed a subtle droop on the right side of his face. There has been no fever, neck stiffness, photophobia, visual changes, or bowel or bladder incontinence. He denies recent travel, sick contacts, or known exposures. He has no history of malignancy, weight loss, night sweats, or chronic cough. He was brought to the emergency department by his wife and subsequently admitted for urgent neurological evaluation and imaging.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 7-8 Hz alpha, mildly slowed compared to expected 9-10 Hz
background.symmetry: Asymmetric; amplitude attenuation and polymorphic slowing over the left parietal and temporal regions
background.reactivity: Present but reduced on the left hemisphere
background.sleep_architecture: Sleep spindles present bilaterally, reduced over left hemisphere

findings.location: Left parietal and posterior temporal regions (P3, T5, C3)
findings.state: Present in wakefulness and persists into drowsiness

findings.morphology: Sharp contour with following slow wave, maximum at P3

findings.location: Bilateral, more prominent over the left hemisphere
findings.morphology: Reduced amplitude and faster frequencies attenuated over left parietal region

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to clinical concern for elevated intracranial pressure
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: No photoparoxysmal response. Normal photic driving at 10-14 Hz posteriorly.

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG. Intermittent epileptiform discharges in the left centro-parietal region indicate cortical irritability and seizure predisposition. Background asymmetry with left hemisphere attenuation further supports a structural etiology. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Hyperventilation not performed due to suspected elevated intracranial pressure.

findings.location: Left parietal lobe, extending medially toward the atrium of the left lateral ventricle and abutting the dura laterally along the convexity

signal_characteristics.T1: Heterogeneously hypointense with areas of intrinsic T1 shortening suggesting hemorrhage or proteinaceous content
signal_characteristics.T2: Heterogeneously hyperintense with areas of low signal centrally consistent with necrosis and hemorrhagic components
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense mass with extensive surrounding FLAIR signal abnormality representing vasogenic edema
signal_characteristics.DWI: Areas of restricted diffusion within the enhancing rim, consistent with high cellularity
signal_characteristics.ADC: Corresponding low ADC values in the enhancing rim; central necrotic areas show facilitated diffusion
signal_characteristics.post_gadolinium: Thick, irregular ring enhancement with nodular enhancing components along the lateral and inferior margins. Areas of central non-enhancement consistent with necrosis.

findings.mass_effect: Compression of the atrium of the left lateral ventricle. Approximately 6 mm rightward midline shift of the septum pellucidum. Effacement of the left parietal sulci. Mild subfalcine herniation.
findings.borders: Irregular and infiltrative margins, indistinct interface with adjacent white matter tracts

findings.location: Left parietal and temporal white matter, extending into the left corona radiata and posterior limb of the internal capsule

signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion in edematous regions

findings.mass_effect: Contributing to overall mass effect and midline shift
findings.borders: Finger-like projections along white matter tracts

volumetrics.lesion_volume: Approximately 58 cm3 including enhancing and necrotic components
volumetrics.edema_volume: Approximately 91 cm3 of perilesional FLAIR signal abnormality

additional_observations: No additional enhancing lesions identified elsewhere in the brain parenchyma
No hydrocephalus aside from mild compression of the left lateral ventricle atrium
Mild mucosal thickening in the left maxillary sinus, incidental
mri.impression: Large heterogeneously enhancing left parietal mass with central necrosis, surrounding vasogenic edema, and mass effect including 6 mm midline shift and early subfalcine herniation. The mass extends medially to the atrium of the left lateral ventricle and abuts the dura laterally. The restricted diffusion in the enhancing rim suggests high cellularity. The dural contact raises concern for dural invasion. Clinical correlation recommended.

ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm with no acute ST-T wave changes. No evidence of arrhythmia, conduction abnormality, or ischemia.

findings.region: Enhancing rim of the left parietal mass
findings.signal: rCBV: Markedly elevated relative cerebral blood volume (rCBV ratio 4.8 relative to contralateral normal-appearing white matter); rCBF: Elevated relative cerebral blood flow in the enhancing component; MTT: Shortened mean transit time in the enhancing rim

findings.region: Central non-enhancing portion of the mass
findings.signal: rCBV: Near-zero rCBV, consistent with necrotic/avascular tissue; rCBF: Absent blood flow centrally

output.impression: See findings above. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.signal: choline: Markedly elevated choline peak (Cho/Cr ratio 3.6, Cho/NAA ratio 5.1); NAA: Markedly reduced N-acetylaspartate (NAA) peak, consistent with neuronal loss/displacement; creatine: Mildly reduced creatine peak; lipid_lactate: Prominent lipid and lactate peaks, consistent with necrosis and anaerobic metabolism

output.impression: MR spectroscopy of the left parietal mass demonstrates markedly elevated choline, reduced NAA, and prominent lipid-lactate peaks. Clinical correlation recommended.

CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis.value: No pulmonary masses or nodules. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. Heart size normal.
Liver, spleen, pancreas, adrenal glands, and kidneys are unremarkable. No focal hepatic lesions. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.
Bladder and prostate are unremarkable. No pelvic lymphadenopathy. No osseous metastatic disease.

Histopathology.value: High-grade glial neoplasm with numerous bizarre multinucleated giant cells, abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm, and pleomorphic nuclei. Brisk mitotic activity with multiple atypical mitotic figures. Extensive areas of palisading necrosis and microvascular proliferation.
Abundant reticulin network surrounding individual and clusters of giant tumor cells

Molecular Markers.value: Wild-type (negative for R132H by IHC, confirmed by sequencing)

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Microscopy High-grade glial neoplasm with numerous bizarre multinucleated giant cells, abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm, and pleomorphic nuclei. Brisk mitotic activity with multiple atypical mitotic figures. Extensive areas of palisading necrosis and microvascular proliferation.  (H), Reticulin stain Abundant reticulin network surrounding individual and clusters of giant tumor cells  (H), GFAP Positive  (H), p53 Strongly positive (>70% nuclear staining)  (H), Ki-67 (MIB-1) 35%  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: High-grade glial neoplasm with giant cell morphology

findings.signal: BOLD: Motor activation is displaced anteriorly and inferiorly by the parietal mass. Primary motor cortex for the right hand is located approximately 1.2 cm anterior to the enhancing margin of the tumor.

findings.region: Left inferior frontal gyrus (Broca area) and left superior temporal gyrus (Wernicke area)
findings.signal: BOLD: Language is strongly lateralized to the left hemisphere (laterality index 0.82). Wernicke area activation is located approximately 2.5 cm inferior and anterior to the tumor margin.

output.impression: Functional MRI demonstrates left hemisphere language dominance and motor cortex displaced anteriorly by the left parietal mass. The primary motor cortex is approximately 1.2 cm from the tumor margin. The corticospinal tract and arcuate fasciculus are displaced but intact. Clinical correlation recommended.

results.title: Giant cell glioblastoma: a clinicopathological study of 25 cases
results.summary: Giant cell glioblastoma (GCG) is a rare WHO grade IV variant accounting for approximately 5% of glioblastomas. Despite its high-grade histology, GCG may carry a slightly better prognosis compared to conventional GBM, with median survival of 14-16 months versus 12-14 months. Treatment follows standard GBM protocols: maximal safe surgical resection followed by concurrent chemoradiation with temozolomide (Stupp protocol).

results.title: Molecular features and prognosis of giant cell glioblastoma
results.authors: Oh JE, Ohta T, et al.
results.summary: GCG is typically IDH wild-type and is characterized by frequent TP53 mutations, low frequency of EGFR amplification, and variable MGMT promoter methylation status. Molecular profiling aids in distinguishing GCG from pleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma (PXA), which has BRAF V600E mutations and different treatment implications. Standard treatment remains maximal safe resection plus Stupp protocol.

results.title: Differentiating giant cell glioblastoma from pleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma
results.authors: Giannini C, Scheithauer BW, et al.
results.summary: Key distinguishing features: GCG shows strong p53 staining, negative neuronal markers (synaptophysin, NeuN), abundant reticulin, and brisk mitoses with necrosis. PXA shows BRAF V600E mutations, positive neuronal antigens, and typically lacks necrosis. Clinical distinction is critical as PXA (WHO grade II-III) has a significantly better prognosis.

output.summary: Giant cell glioblastoma is a rare variant of GBM (WHO grade IV) with slightly better prognosis than conventional GBM. Standard treatment follows the Stupp protocol: maximal safe surgical resection followed by concurrent radiation therapy (60 Gy in 30 fractions) and temozolomide, then adjuvant temozolomide. Molecular profiling (IDH, MGMT, TP53) is essential. Key differential diagnosis is pleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma, distinguished by immunohistochemistry (p53+, synaptophysin-negative in GCG) and molecular markers (BRAF V600E in PXA). Despite the giant cell morphology suggesting a unique variant, treatment principles remain aligned with standard GBM management.

output.proposed: Temozolomide 75 mg/m2 daily during radiation, then 150-200 mg/m2 days 1-5 every 28 days
interactions: Levetiracetam: No significant pharmacokinetic interaction. Levetiracetam is a preferred antiepileptic in brain tumor patients because it does not induce hepatic CYP enzymes, unlike phenytoin or carbamazepine, which can reduce temozolomide levels.
warnings: Monitor CBC weekly during concurrent chemoradiation phase and before each adjuvant cycle
Pneumocystis jirovecii prophylaxis (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) required during concurrent chemoradiation phase
Hepatotoxicity possible — monitor liver function tests
Myelosuppression is dose-limiting; dose reduction or delay may be needed based on nadir counts
Antiemetics (ondansetron) recommended prior to each dose
output.formulary_status: Formulary approved; standard of care for high-grade gliomas per NCCN guidelines
alternatives: Lomustine (CCNU) — alternative alkylating agent if temozolomide is not tolerated
Bevacizumab — anti-VEGF monoclonal antibody for recurrent GBM
Tumor Treating Fields (TTFields/Optune) — approved adjunct to adjuvant temozolomide

output.proposed: Dexamethasone 4 mg IV every 6 hours for perilesional edema and mass effect
interactions: Amlodipine: Corticosteroids may counteract antihypertensive effect by promoting sodium and fluid retention; monitor blood pressure closely.
Levetiracetam: No significant interaction. Dexamethasone does not alter levetiracetam levels.
warnings: Hyperglycemia: Dexamethasone can significantly elevate blood glucose; monitor glucose closely, especially given mildly elevated baseline fasting glucose
Gastric irritation: Administer with PPI (pantoprazole) or H2-blocker for gastroprotection
Immunosuppression: Increases infection risk; monitor for signs of opportunistic infection
Myopathy: Prolonged high-dose steroids can cause proximal muscle weakness (steroid myopathy)
Taper gradually after surgery; avoid abrupt discontinuation
output.formulary_status: Formulary approved; standard treatment for brain tumor-associated vasogenic edema
alternatives: Methylprednisolone — alternative corticosteroid, less commonly used for brain edema
Bevacizumab — steroid-sparing anti-edema option in refractory cases

differential.key_distinguishing: PXA shows BRAF V600E mutations, positive neuronal antigens (synaptophysin, NeuN), and typically lacks palisading necrosis; GCG is p53-positive and neuronal marker-negative
SEGA is associated with tuberous sclerosis complex (absent in this patient), located near the foramen of Monro, and is a WHO grade I tumor with different histological features

Clinical and radiological differentiation from conventional GBM is difficult; diagnosis relies on histopathological identification of numerous multinucleated giant cells, abundant reticulin, and specific immunohistochemical profile

Solitary metastasis typically has more spherical shape, well-demarcated borders at gray-white junction, and requires a known primary malignancy; CT chest/abdomen/pelvis negative for extracranial primary
Abscess shows restricted diffusion centrally (pus), thin smooth ring enhancement, and typically presents with fever and elevated WBC; this patient is afebrile with normal WBC

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI with gadolinium contrast to characterize the intracranial lesion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Large heterogeneously enhancing left parietal mass with central necrosis, ring enhancement, perilesional edema, and midline shift

optimal_actions.action: Order routine laboratory studies including CBC, BMP, coagulation panel, and liver function tests as pre-surgical baseline
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal CBC, BMP, coagulation, and liver function; no contraindication to surgery

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG to evaluate seizure risk and characterize epileptiform activity given history of seizures
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Focal slowing over left parietal region with epileptiform discharges in peri-tumoral cortex

optimal_actions.action: Initiate dexamethasone for perilesional edema and mass effect reduction
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Dexamethasone appropriate; monitor glucose and add gastroprotection

optimal_actions.action: Request MRI perfusion to further characterize the lesion and support high-grade glioma diagnosis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated rCBV in enhancing rim consistent with hypervascular high-grade tumor

optimal_actions.action: Obtain CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to rule out extracranial primary malignancy
Obtain pre-surgical functional MRI for motor and language cortex mapping
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Motor cortex displaced but not infiltrated; language lateralized to left hemisphere

optimal_actions.action: Refer for maximal safe surgical resection with intraoperative neuronavigation and tissue for histopathology and molecular testing
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Giant cell glioblastoma on histopathology with IDH wild-type, MGMT unmethylated

optimal_actions.action: Plan adjuvant concurrent chemoradiation with temozolomide (Stupp protocol) after surgical recovery
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Temozolomide compatible with current medications; PCP prophylaxis required

critical_actions: Obtain urgent brain MRI with gadolinium contrast to characterize the mass lesion
Administer dexamethasone to reduce perilesional edema and prevent further herniation given 6 mm midline shift and papilledema
Continue and optimize antiepileptic therapy given active epileptiform discharges
Refer for urgent neurosurgical consultation for maximal safe resection
Obtain tissue diagnosis with histopathology and molecular markers (IDH, MGMT, 1p/19q) to guide treatment
contraindicated_actions: Lumbar puncture — contraindicated due to elevated intracranial pressure with midline shift and early herniation
Withholding dexamethasone in the setting of significant mass effect and midline shift
Administering enzyme-inducing antiepileptics (phenytoin, carbamazepine) as they reduce temozolomide efficacy
Stereotactic radiosurgery alone without tissue diagnosis
Delaying neurosurgical referral in the setting of progressive neurological deficits and mass effect
key_reasoning_points: The prior episode diagnosed as 'viral encephalitis' nine months ago with a subtle hyperdense left parietal lesion on CT likely represents an earlier stage of the same neoplasm, highlighting the importance of re-evaluating prior diagnoses when new findings emerge

Rapid clinical progression from initial seizure to hemiparesis over nine months, combined with aggressive imaging features (ring enhancement, necrosis, mass effect), strongly suggests a high-grade malignancy
The EEG pattern of continuous focal delta activity with epileptiform discharges localizes to the left parietal region, correlating with the MRI-identified mass and guiding lesion localization

Differentiating giant cell glioblastoma from pleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma requires immunohistochemistry: GCG is p53-positive and neuronal marker-negative, while PXA shows BRAF V600E and positive neuronal antigens
Subependymal giant cell astrocytoma is excluded by the absence of tuberous sclerosis features and the high-grade histology with necrosis and brisk mitotic activity

IDH wild-type status and MGMT promoter unmethylation classify this as a primary (de novo) GBM with guarded prognosis, guiding treatment with standard Stupp protocol despite reduced expected temozolomide benefit
Dural abutment of the mass is an important surgical consideration but does not change the diagnosis — GBM can invade dura despite being a primary intra-axial tumor

metadata.condition_name: Brain tumor — high-grade glioma (giant cell glioblastoma)
metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward case with classic imaging features of high-grade glioma (ring enhancement, necrosis, mass effect, midline shift) and rapid clinical progression. The prior misdiagnosis of viral encephalitis adds clinical realism but does not significantly complicate the current diagnostic workup.
